title: The Two Genealogies of Jesus
subtitle: Why Matthew and Luke's family trees diverge, and what this means for Christian and Islamic understandings of Jesus's identity
summary: The Gospels of Matthew and Luke present strikingly different genealogies for Jesus, raising the question: if Jesus had no earthly father, why trace Joseph's lineage at all? Christian scholars offer several explanations, while the Quran's silence on genealogy—presenting Jesus instead through Mary's lineage—reflects a distinct theological framework. Both traditions affirm Jesus's miraculous origins, but diverge on how family connection matters.

seo.metaTitle: The Two Genealogies of Jesus: Matthew vs Luke
seo.metaDescription: Explore why Matthew and Luke present different genealogies for Jesus, Christian scholarly responses to the discrepancy, and how the Quran addresses Jesus's lineage.

sections.title: The Genealogical Problem
sections.body: Matthew and Luke both open their gospel narratives with genealogies meant to establish Jesus's place in Jewish history and prophecy. Yet these genealogies diverge dramatically. From King David forward, they name almost entirely different ancestors. Matthew traces Jesus through Solomon (David's son), while Luke traces him through Nathan (David's other son). Matthew's genealogy spans 42 generations; Luke's spans 77. Most strikingly for modern readers: if Jesus was born of a virgin—a doctrine affirmed in both gospels—why does either genealogy matter? They trace the lineage of Joseph, who Christians hold was not Jesus's biological father. This apparent logical tension has prompted centuries of scholarly explanation.

sections.title: Matthew's Genealogical Framework
sections.body: Matthew opens with an explicit genealogical statement: "The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham." This verse announces Matthew's theological intent—to place Jesus firmly within the covenant lineage. Matthew traces the line from Abraham through David, emphasizing the royal line through Solomon. A key moment comes at Matthew 1:6, which notes: "Jesse became the father of King David. David became the father of Solomon by her who had been Uriah’s wife." The reference to Bathsheba (identified by her former marriage to Uriah) is theologically significant—it reminds readers that even the line of Israel's greatest king included scandal and divine forgiveness. Matthew culminates the genealogy at 1:16: "Jacob became the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ." The phrasing is deliberate: Joseph is named as the legal (not biological) father, yet the genealogy is structured to establish Jesus's legal claim to the throne of David.

sections.title: Luke's Genealogical Framework
sections.body: Luke presents his genealogy differently. He situates it after Jesus's baptism and temptation, immediately before Jesus begins his public ministry. Luke 3:23 introduces: "Jesus himself, when he began to teach, was about thirty years old, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli," The phrase "as was supposed" subtly signals awareness that this is not biological fatherhood. From Joseph, Luke traces backward through generations to King David and beyond. Luke 3:31 names the line through David's son Nathan: "the son of Melea, the son of Menan, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David," This divergence from Matthew is complete—not merely different names, but a fundamentally different branch of the family tree. Luke extends the genealogy all the way back to Adam, concluding at 3:38: "the son of Enos, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God." The final phrase, "the son of God," suggests that Luke's genealogy, by reaching to Adam (who in tradition was created directly by God), positions Jesus within all humanity and points toward his divine sonship.

sections.title: The Core Tensions
sections.body: Several tensions immediately strike the reader: First, the genealogies are largely incompatible. From David onward, Matthew and Luke share almost no names. Matthew places Joseph's father as Jacob; Luke places him as Heli. Matthew's line descends through Solomon; Luke's through Nathan. If both accounts aimed at historical accuracy, they cannot both be correct. Second, the virgin birth complicates any genealogical argument. If Jesus was born of Mary without a biological father, what genealogical claim does Joseph's ancestry establish? Matthew and Luke both affirm the virgin birth—yet they build elaborate genealogies of Joseph. Third, Matthew's genealogy includes scandalous figures and foreign women (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba), suggesting that the genealogy is not merely a historical record but a theological narrative about grace working through broken families. Luke's backward extension to Adam and God emphasizes universal humanity and divine origin. These differing emphases suggest the genealogies serve different theological purposes rather than simply recording identical historical facts.

sections.title: Christian Scholarly Responses
sections.body: Christian scholars have developed several explanations for the divergent genealogies. The most widespread interpretation distinguishes between legal and biological lineage. According to this view, Matthew presents Joseph's line (and thus Jesus's legal lineage as heir to David's throne), while Luke presents Mary's lineage (Jesus's biological ancestry). Support for this interpretation comes from early church tradition. The 3rd-century apologist Julius Africanus (preserved in Eusebius, Ecclesiastical History 1.7) provided one harmonization account: Joseph's grandfather Matthan and grandfather Melchi (via Luke's line) married the same woman, Estha, in succession; her sons by different husbands, Jacob and Heli, were thus half-brothers. When Heli died childless, Jacob married his widow and fathered Joseph—naturally Jacob's son but legally Heli's son, thus appearing in both genealogies through different relationships. In Africanus's account, both genealogies trace Joseph's line, not Mary's. A distinct theory—that Heli was Mary's father and Luke traces the maternal line—emerged later, popularized in the 15th century by the scholar Annius of Viterbo and later adopted by some Reformation-era and subsequent commentators. This theory is sometimes conflated with Africanus's account, though they differ fundamentally in their mechanisms and in whether they place Mary or Joseph at the genealogy's center. A second explanation posits that one genealogy is theological composition rather than historical record. Matthew's deliberate structuring into three groups of 14 generations (emphasizing 14, which in Hebrew numerology equals the letters of David's name), and his inclusion of narrative-relevant figures, suggests Matthew shaped his genealogy to teach theological points rather than preserve unalterable historical detail. A third approach acknowledges that ancient genealogies sometimes compressed or adapted lineages for various purposes—to connect figures theologically, to establish covenantal continuity, or to teach moral lessons. By this view, both Matthew and Luke drew on genuine traditions but adapted them. A fourth interpretation notes that Matthew's genealogy emphasizes Jesus's kingship (son of David), while Luke's emphasizes his universal humanity (son of Adam, son of God). These different purposes would naturally produce different emphases and possibly different source material. Most scholars today recognize that the genealogies are not intended as modern historical documentation but as theological statements using genealogical form. This does not mean they are false, but rather that they were shaped to convey religious meaning.

sections.title: The Quranic Approach to Genealogy
sections.body: The Quran's treatment of Jesus's genealogy stands in marked contrast to the Gospel accounts. Rather than tracing a paternal line, the Quran consistently identifies Jesus through his mother, Mary. In Surah Ali 'Imran 3:33, the Quran states: "Indeed, Allah chose Adam and Noah and the family of Abraham and the family of 'Imran over the worlds," establishing Mary as part of the honored lineage of 'Imran's family—a lineage chosen and elevated by God. Most directly, Quran 3:45 announces: "[And mention] when the angels said, "O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah]." The Quranic identification is consistent: Jesus is 'Isa ibn Maryam—Jesus, son of Mary. This identification serves multiple purposes in Islamic theology. First, it emphasizes Jesus's genuine humanity—he had a mother, belonged to a human family, was born in time and space. Second, it avoids any suggestion of biological paternity, whether through Joseph or any other man. Third, by identifying him through Mary, the Quran honors her as the vessel of this miraculous conception. The Quranic emphasis on Mary's role is theologically profound: it affirms Jesus's humanity while maintaining his miraculous origin, without needing to establish a legal or biological patrilineal claim. One interpretive question that arises in scholarly literature concerns the identity of "'Imran's family" in Quranic usage: elsewhere in the Quranic and biblical chronology, 'Imran (Amram) is identified as the father of Moses and Aaron, figures roughly 1,300+ years before Mary's time. Some scholars and commentators have proposed varying resolutions—that the references are to different families sharing the name, that genealogical frameworks in antiquity worked differently than modern assumptions, or that the Quranic identification is primarily theological rather than genealogical. Muslim scholars of the classical and modern periods have offered a range of interpretations on this point. There is no genealogical problem in the Quranic framework because the Quran does not claim Jesus as heir to David's throne through a paternal genealogy. Instead, it presents him as a prophet and messenger chosen by God, with Mary's honored place in the lineage of believers being sufficient genealogical context.

sections.title: Theological Divergence: Kingship, Prophetic Office, and Lineage
sections.body: The genealogical differences reflect deeper theological divergences between Christian and Islamic understandings of Jesus. In Christian theology, Jesus's connection to David is crucial because Jesus is understood as the Messiah—the promised king. Messianic expectation in Jewish tradition held that the Messiah would be from David's line, would restore the kingdom, and would reign. Matthew's genealogy directly addresses this: by establishing Jesus's connection to David through Joseph (and through Mary, in the Christian reading that identifies Luke's line as Mary's), Matthew asserts that Jesus fulfills the kingly Messianic expectation. The genealogy is part of Matthew's argument that Jesus is the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy regarding a Davidic king. Luke's backward extension to Adam and God widens the frame: Jesus is not merely king of Israel, but savior of all humanity, connected to the whole human family and to God himself. In Islamic theology, Jesus holds a different role. He is a prophet and messenger, one of the greatest and most honored. But he is not a king in the political sense, and his significance does not depend on a Davidic genealogy. Instead, his significance derives from his prophetic office, his miraculous birth, his moral teaching, and his signs. The Quranic emphasis on Mary (rather than Joseph) reflects this: what matters is not a political claim through patrilineal descent, but the miraculous conception itself—God caused Jesus to come into being as a word from Him. The genealogy, in the Islamic view, is less theologically central because Jesus's legitimacy rests not on descent but on divine selection. This reflects a different conception of authority and legitimacy: Christian tradition roots it partly in covenantal continuity and royal descent; Islamic tradition roots it in direct divine choice and prophetic office. Neither genealogy, by this analysis, is merely factual reporting; both are shaped by theological commitments about who Jesus is and what his significance means.

sections.title: Unresolved Questions and Shared Ground
sections.body: The divergent genealogies of Matthew and Luke remain unexplained by any single scholarly theory that commands universal acceptance. Whether one interprets them as legal vs. biological lines, or as theological composition, or as differently sourced traditions that were not harmonized, the fact of their divergence stands. What both gospels affirm clearly—and what separates them from historical skepticism—is Jesus's connection to Israel's covenantal story and to humanity itself. Both present Jesus as genuinely human, born into a family, rooted in history. The Quranic approach sidesteps the genealogical puzzle altogether by focusing on Mary rather than Joseph, emphasizing the miraculous conception and prophetic office over questions of inheritance and kingly succession. For Christian tradition, Jesus's Davidic descent (however traced) matters because it connects him to messianic hope and covenantal promise. For Islamic tradition, Jesus's place in the honored family of believers (through Mary) and his status as a chosen messenger matter more than patrilineal descent. Both traditions affirm that Jesus was born miraculously, without a human father. Both traditions honor Jesus as a central figure in God's plan for humanity. They differ in how lineage and succession relate to that central significance, and those differences reflect their different theological frameworks about Jesus's role and identity.

bibleVerses.text: The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.
Jesse became the father of King David. David became the father of Solomon by her who had been Uriah’s wife.
Jacob became the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.
Jesus himself, when he began to teach, was about thirty years old, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli,
the son of Melea, the son of Menan, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David,
the son of Enos, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.

faq.question: If Jesus had no biological father, why do the gospels include genealogies through Joseph?
faq.answer: Christian scholars offer several explanations. The most widespread holds that Joseph's genealogy establishes Jesus's legal status and claim to David's throne and the Messianic promise. Matthew explicitly frames genealogy as establishing Messianic lineage ("son of David, son of Abraham"). Some scholars propose that Luke's genealogy actually traces Mary's biological line through her father Heli, preserved via levirate marriage customs. Others argue the genealogies are theological statements rather than purely historical records—they serve to connect Jesus to Israel's covenantal story and to position him within God's plan, not merely to document ancestry. The phrase in Luke 3:23, "as was supposed," explicitly signals that this is not biological fatherhood, suggesting the evangelists were aware of the theological rather than merely genealogical function.

faq.question: Why are Matthew's and Luke's genealogies so different? Is one of them wrong?
faq.answer: The genealogies diverge dramatically—different names, different lengths, different branches from David. Scholars debate whether this reflects different sources, theological purposes, or both. Neither Matthew nor Luke claims to be providing the same genealogy; they may have drawn from different family traditions or sources. Matthew emphasizes the royal line through Solomon and frames Jesus as fulfilling kingly Messianic expectations. Luke extends the genealogy back to Adam and presents Jesus in relation to all humanity and to God. Rather than one being "wrong," they may reflect different theological emphases. Modern scholars generally view genealogies in ancient texts, including biblical ones, as shaped by theological purpose as well as historical memory. This does not mean they are fabricated, but rather that they were structured to convey religious meaning.

faq.question: What does the Quran say about Jesus's genealogy?
faq.answer: The Quran does not provide an extended genealogy for Jesus. Instead, it consistently identifies him as 'Isa ibn Maryam (Jesus, son of Mary), emphasizing his connection through his mother rather than a patrilineal line. Quran 3:45 announces that the angels told Mary she would bear Jesus, "the son of Mary." This Quranic approach affirms Jesus's genuine humanity and his miraculous origin without needing to establish a genealogical claim to kingly succession. The emphasis on Mary honors her role in Jesus's conception and elevates her as part of the chosen family of believers (Quran 3:33). In Islamic theology, Jesus's authority derives from his prophetic office and divine selection, not from genealogical inheritance.

faq.question: What is levirate marriage, and how does it explain the genealogies?
faq.answer: Levirate marriage was an ancient Israelite practice in which a man married the widow of his childless brother to preserve the family line and inheritance. The child born to such a union was legally considered the son of the deceased brother. The 3rd-century Christian apologist Julius Africanus used levirate marriage to explain the genealogies: according to his account, Joseph's half-brothers Jacob (Matthew's line) and Heli (Luke's line) were sons of the same mother but different fathers, married in succession. When Heli died childless, Jacob married his widow and fathered Joseph. Thus Joseph could be counted in both genealogies—as the natural son of Jacob (Matthew's genealogy) and as the legal son of Heli through levirate inheritance (Luke's genealogy). In this theory, both genealogies trace Joseph's line but through different family relationships. A separate, later theory proposes that Luke's genealogy actually traces Mary's line, with Heli as her father. This theory emerged centuries after Africanus and is often conflated with his account, though the mechanisms differ. While both theories attempted to harmonize Matthew and Luke, they depend on assumptions not explicitly stated in the gospels themselves and remain disputed among scholars.

faq.question: Why does Matthew include scandalous figures in the genealogy?
faq.answer: Matthew's genealogy explicitly mentions Tamar (who became pregnant with her father-in-law Judah through deception), Rahab (a Canaanite prostitute), Ruth (a Moabite foreigner, from a people generally viewed negatively in Israelite tradition), and Bathsheba (identified by her former husband Uriah, in reference to King David's adultery with her). These inclusions appear intentional. They suggest that Matthew's genealogy is not merely documenting royal lineage but is narrating a theological story about grace—God's purposes worked through broken families, scandal, and unlikely people. By including these figures, Matthew communicates that Jesus's line is not one of pristine purity but of God working redemptively through human sin and failure. This theological point about grace and divine purpose may explain why Matthew shapes his genealogy differently than Luke, who does not include such narrative texture.

faq.question: How do these genealogical accounts affect belief in the virgin birth?
faq.answer: The genealogies do not undermine the virgin birth; both Matthew and Luke explicitly affirm Jesus's conception without a human father, and both immediately follow their genealogies with accounts of the virgin conception. The genealogies function differently: they establish Jesus's connection to Israel's covenantal history and to the Messianic promise. In Matthew's framework, Joseph (the legal father) establishes the genealogical connection; the genealogy need not be biological to be meaningful. In Luke's framework, similarly, the genealogy establishes Jesus within human history and covenantal tradition. The phrase in Luke 3:23, "being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph," explicitly notes that this was a matter of assumption, not biological fact. Thus the genealogies and the virgin birth account operate on different theological levels: the genealogy addresses covenantal continuity and Messianic expectation; the virgin conception addresses the miraculous nature of Jesus's origin.

furtherReading.title: Christianity and Rabbinic Judaism: A Parallel History of Their Origins and Early Development
furtherReading.author: Hershel Shanks (ed.)
furtherReading.note: Edited volume contextualizing Jewish genealogical practices and expectations regarding Messianic descent; includes a chapter by James H. Charlesworth, "Christians and Jews in the First Six Centuries."

furtherReading.title: The Birth of the Messiah: A Commentary on the Infancy Narratives in the Gospels of Matthew and Luke
furtherReading.author: Raymond E. Brown
furtherReading.note: Comprehensive scholarly treatment of Matthew and Luke's genealogies, sources, and theological purposes.

furtherReading.title: The Purpose of the Biblical Genealogies: With Special Reference to the Setting of the Genealogies of Jesus
furtherReading.author: Marshall D. Johnson
furtherReading.note: A dedicated academic monograph analyzing the form and function of biblical genealogies, with a major focus on reconciling and interpreting Matthew's and Luke's genealogies of Jesus.

furtherReading.title: Jesus in the Qur'an
furtherReading.author: Geoffrey Parrinder
furtherReading.note: Examines the Quranic presentation of Jesus and Mary, including genealogical considerations and theological significance.
